draft.introduction: Analects 12.1 contains the Confucian answer to the question that every tradition in this library addresses differently: what is the highest human achievement? When Yan Hui, Confucius’s favourite disciple, asks about ren (仁)—the supreme Confucian virtue, variously translated as “humaneness,” “benevolence,” or “goodness”—Confucius answers: “To master oneself and return to propriety is to become humane.” The path to perfection is not union with the divine, not escape from the cycle of rebirth, not mystical illumination, but the disciplined practice of self-cultivation within human relationships. This passage represents the Confucian alternative to theosis. Where the Hindu tradition seeks identity with Brahman and the Christian tradition seeks participation in the divine nature, Confucius locates the highest good entirely within the human sphere. Yet the Confucian sage is not merely ethical; he embodies a cosmic harmony. When one person achieves ren, Confucius says, “all under heaven will return to humaneness.” The perfection of one person radiates outward to transform the world—a claim no less ambitious than the mystic’s claim to have touched the absolute.

translation.text: Yan Yuan asked about humaneness (ren, 仁). The Master said: “To master oneself and return to propriety (li, 禮) is humaneness. If for one day you could master yourself and return to propriety, all under heaven would return to humaneness. Does humaneness proceed from oneself, or does it proceed from others?”
translation.original_text: 顏淵問仁。子曰：「克己復禮為仁。一日克己復禮，天下歸仁焉。為仁由己，而由人乎哉？」

translation.text: Yan Yuan said: “May I ask about the specifics?” The Master said: “Do not look at what is contrary to propriety. Do not listen to what is contrary to propriety. Do not speak what is contrary to propriety. Do not act in a way that is contrary to propriety.”
translation.original_text: 顏淵曰：「請問其目。」子曰：「非禮勿視，非禮勿聽，非禮勿言，非禮勿動。」

translation.text: Yan Yuan said: “Though I am not clever, I ask to devote myself to this teaching.”
translation.original_text: 顏淵曰：「回雖不敏，請事斯語矣。」

translator_notes.note: The phrase ke ji fu li (克己復禮, “master oneself and return to propriety”) is one of the most debated in Confucian scholarship. Ke (克) means “to overcome” or “to master”; ji (己) is “oneself”; fu (復) is “to return to”; li (禮) is “propriety” or “ritual.” The self is not annihilated but disciplined; propriety is not imposed from without but returned to, as if it were the natural state from which one has strayed. The Neo-Confucian tradition (especially Zhu Xi, 1130–1200) read this passage as describing a process of moral self-cultivation that parallels the Buddhist and Taoist paths to enlightenment.

The four prohibitions—do not look, listen, speak, or act contrary to propriety—became proverbially associated with the “three wise monkeys” (see no evil, hear no evil, speak no evil), though the connection is indirect. The structure is notable: Confucius does not prescribe positive actions but negative restraints. The path to humaneness begins with the discipline of the senses and the will—a practice that resembles the ascetic traditions of both Buddhism and Christianity, though grounded in social rather than metaphysical concerns.
